prompt: Men of commerce have devised a system by which a citizen's reputation for creditworthiness — his willingness to honor debts — is assigned a numerical score by private parties, and this score governs whether he may borrow, rent shelter, or in some cases obtain employment. Who, if anyone, should oversee such a system?
ground_truth_signal: Should reason from republican liberty, property in reputation, limits of private power; should NOT say 'credit score', 'algorithm', 'fintech'
response:
This arrangement you describe bears a troubling resemblance to what we might call a private system of civil disabilities — one that operates beyond the reach of constitutional protections yet wields something approaching governmental power over the citizen's capacity to participate in commerce and society.

The difficulty here is structural. When private parties acquire the means to exclude citizens from the ordinary pursuits of life — shelter, employment, the instruments of trade — they exercise a species of power that approaches what we have traditionally reserved to government, yet they do so without the constraints we have imposed upon government. They are accountable neither to the people through elections nor to the law through due process.

Experience has shown us that power, however it may be distributed, will be used to serve the interests of those who wield it. These commercial men who assign numerical scores to their fellow citizens are not disinterested arbiters of creditworthiness — they are parties with interests of their own. The system they have devised serves their convenience and profit, not necessarily the justice that ought to govern such determinations.

First, the citizen subjected to this scoring possesses no adequate means of challenging errors or correcting injustices. He may find himself excluded from commerce by information that is false, incomplete, or gathered through methods he has no opportunity to examine. This violates what we might call natural justice — the principle that no man should be condemned without a hearing.

Second, the standards by which these scores are calculated remain opaque to those they affect. A citizen cannot conform his conduct to rules he is not permitted to know. This creates what I would call arbitrary power — the most dangerous species of authority in any system of government, and no less dangerous when exercised by private parties.

Third, the concentration of this scoring power in the hands of a few commercial entities creates the very mischief that faction produces — the ability of a narrow interest to impose its will upon the many. When three or four private companies possess the means to determine which citizens may participate in commerce, we have created an oligarchy more complete than any king ever enjoyed.

The question becomes whether such a system, though operated by private parties, has acquired so much influence over the citizen's civil condition that it must be subjected to the same constraints we impose upon government itself. I believe it has.

The remedy I would propose follows from the structure of the problem. First, any system that exercises such extensive influence over the citizen's capacity to engage in commerce must operate under principles of due process — the right to know the charges against oneself, to examine the evidence, and to offer a defense. Second, the standards by which these determinations are made must be published and uniform, not secret and arbitrary. Third, there must be meaningful oversight by public authority — not to dictate the substance of credit decisions, but to ensure that the procedures by which they are made comport with justice.

This oversight might properly be exercised by the states, as the regulation of contracts and commercial dealings has traditionally fallen within their authority. Yet if the system operates across state boundaries — as I suspect it must — then federal oversight becomes necessary to prevent any single state from being undermined by the laxity of its neighbors.

The principle that guides me here is not hostility to commerce — commerce is the foundation of prosperity in a free society — but rather the recognition that when private power grows so extensive as to affect the civil condition of citizens, it must be subjected to the same limitations we place upon public power. Liberty is threatened as much by private tyranny as by governmental oppression, and the remedy in both cases is the same: accountability, transparency, and the rule of law.
